title: Dark chocolate bark
subtitle: Thin chocolate slab with nuts, dried fruit, and sea salt
excerpt: Melted dark chocolate poured thin onto baking paper, scattered with toppings of your choice, and left to set. No thermometer or cooking required beyond melting the chocolate.
sourceNotes: Chocolate bark is a domestic confection with no single documented origin. The technique of pouring melted chocolate flat and scattering toppings before setting is found in home confectionery manuals from the early twentieth century. The toppings vary by season and preference; the method is unchanged.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a single layer between sheets of baking paper. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Scales directly. Use a larger tray for bigger batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a cool dry place for 2 weeks. Do not refrigerate in humid conditions: the chocolate will bloom.

content.text: Dark chocolate bark is confectionery at its simplest: melt, pour, scatter, set. The only decision is toppings. Sea salt flakes and roasted almonds give a clean contrast to the chocolate. Dried cranberries and pistachios give colour. Candied orange peel and flaked almonds work for a Christmas version. The method is the same regardless.

Use chocolate with at least 70% cocoa solids for bark: the higher cocoa butter content means it sets harder and breaks cleanly. Lower-percentage chocolate tends to stay too soft at room temperature. If you want a shinier, crisper result without a full tempering process, melt two-thirds of the chocolate, remove from the heat, and stir in the remaining third chopped small: the unmelted chocolate seeds the crystal structure.

Line a large baking tray with baking paper. Put two-thirds of the {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, making sure the base of the bowl doesn't touch the water. Stir occasionally until just melted. Remove from the heat and stir in the remaining chocolate. Keep stirring until all the chocolate has melted and the mixture has cooled slightly and thickened.

Pour the chocolate onto the lined tray and spread with a palette knife or offset spatula to a thin, even layer about 3-4 mm thick. Scatter your chosen toppings over the surface immediately, pressing them lightly into the chocolate. Leave to set at room temperature for at least 1 hour, or until completely firm. Break into rough pieces by hand.
